narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 45% chance that you might not pass the bar exam on your first try, which is above our threshold for approval. This prediction is based on your overall profile, including your third-year school decile rank of 3, first-year decile of 3, undergraduate GPA of 2.50, LSAT score of 28, upper-middle family income bracket, being a full-time student, female, and Hispanic background. Compared to typical students in our dataset—who often have third-year deciles around 5.5, first-year around 5.7, LSAT scores near 37, and GPAs over 3.2—your academic metrics stand out as notably lower, placing you in the lower range of performers. The model's decision was influenced most strongly by your third-year decile rank, which pushed the prediction toward a higher risk of bar failure more than any other factor. Right behind that was your LSAT score, which also contributed toward the failure prediction by being well below average. Your first-year decile rank came next in influence, similarly nudging the odds against passing. Family income, racial background, undergraduate GPA, gender, and full-time status had progressively smaller roles, all leaning slightly toward the riskier outcome but not as decisively. While demographics like being female or Hispanic are less common in the dataset (where most are white males), they weren't the main drivers here—your grades and test scores carried the heaviest weight. This ranking shows how past academic performance dominated the model's thinking for your case. To improve your chances next time, focus on what you can realistically boost, like retaking the LSAT to aim closer to the typical 37, or strengthening any future coursework to lift those decile ranks. Building study habits proven to help bar passage, perhaps through targeted prep courses, could also shift future predictions in your favor. You're already planning full-time study, which aligns with most successful applicants, so lean into that strength. With targeted improvements in your weaker areas, you could realign your profile toward the passing predictions we see for approved students. Keep pushing—you have the potential to turn this around.
